title: Fast, powerful, low-noise optical pumping of an atomic vapor with semiconductor optical amplifiers
authors: Diana Méndez-Avalos, Théo Louzada Meireles, Morgan W. Mitchell, Aleksandra Sierant
abstract: We use a $^{87}\text{Rb}$ atomic vapor, suitable for an optically-pumped magnetometer (OPM) in Earth-field conditions, to study the noise properties of three strategies for generating pulsed optical pumping. We compare a frequency-modulated (FM) laser, amplitude modulation (AM) via an acousto-optic modulator (AOM), and amplitude modulation via a semiconductor optical amplifier (SOA). Pumping the ensemble to operate as a Bell-Bloom OPM, and with an equal degree of spin polarization, the three methods give nearly identical sensitivity, showing that the SOA, despite being an active device, can introduce negligible additional noise. Pumping the ensemble to operate as a free-induction-decay OPM, we observe longer unpumped coherence times with the SOA-AM method than with the FM method. Finally, using the higher power available from the SOA, we demonstrate an environment-limited sensitivity of $80\text{fT}/\sqrt{\text{Hz}}$ at $600\text{Hz}$ and 200fT$200\text{fT}/\sqrt{\text{Hz}}$ at $4\text{kHz}$, one to two orders of magnitude beyond what was achievable with the other pumping methods.
summary: This paper investigates using a semiconductor optical amplifier (SOA) to perform high-power, low-noise pulsed optical pumping in an $^{87}$Rb atomic vapor magnetometer. By comparing SOA-based amplitude modulation to traditional frequency and amplitude modulation, the authors show that the SOA introduces negligible extra noise while providing much higher power. This approach enables magnetic sensitivities up to two orders of magnitude better than previous methods and preserves spin coherence more effectively. The results suggest SOA-AM is a powerful tool for next-generation high-sensitivity atomic sensors.
main_problem: Evaluating the noise properties and performance of different pulsed optical pumping strategies, specifically comparing semiconductor optical amplifier (SOA) amplitude modulation against traditional FM and AOM-AM methods for atomic magnetometry.
main_result: The SOA-AM method introduces negligible additional noise and achieves significantly higher magnetic sensitivity (up to 80 fT/sqrt(Hz)) and longer unpumped coherence times compared to FM and AOM-AM methods.
method: The researchers compared three modulation techniques (FM, AOM-AM, and SOA-AM) in a Bell-Bloom and FID-mode $^{87}$Rb magnetometer, measuring Faraday rotation, spin polarization, and magnetic sensitivity.
model_or_system: An experimental platform consisting of an $^{87}$Rb atomic vapor in a 3 cm glass cell with 100 torr N2 buffer gas, operated in Earth-field conditions using a Bell-Bloom and Free-Induction-Decay (FID) magnetometer setup.
key_observables: Faraday rotation angle, spin polarization (Pz), coherence time (T2), and magnetic-field sensitivity (fT/sqrt(Hz)).
important_parameters: Larmor frequencies of ~500 kHz, duty cycles of 10%, and sensitivities reaching 80 fT/sqrt(Hz) at 600 Hz.
main_assumptions: The sensitivity at higher pump powers is limited by environmental noise rather than the SOA itself.
figures_summary: Fig 1a shows the Bell-Bloom OPM setup; Fig 3a shows the PSD of polarization noise under SOA-AM; Fig 3b compares total spin noise between FM and SOA-AM.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of pulsed optical pumping, describes the experimental setup and three modulation methods, presents a comparative noise and coherence analysis, and concludes with the demonstrated sensitivity improvements.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for quantum optics and AMO physicists as it demonstrates a way to achieve high-power, low-noise control of atomic spin states, which is critical for developing high-bandwidth quantum sensors and interfaces.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Bell–Bloom OPM setup. 87Rb vapor cell, heater, and coils are enclosed within a three-layer mu-metal shield. The three different pump laser sources are presented in the or- ange dotted boxes: the frequency modulation (FM) scheme is implemented by controlling the current injected into the laser; the semiconductor optical amplifier-based amplitude modu- lation (SOA-AM) scheme is implemented by modulating the current injected into the BOA; while for the acousto-optic modulator-based amplitude modulation (AOM-AM) scheme, the modulation is implemented by controlling the voltage of the VVA that controls the attenuation of the 1st-order diffracted beam. BOA - booster optical amplifier,...

FIG. 2. a) BOA characterization setup. A waveform gen- erator (WFG) provides the modulation signal to the BOA current driver. The BOA is seeded by a distributed Bragg reflector (DBR) laser, and its temperature is stabilized using a thermoelectric controller (TEC). The amplified and modu- lated light is sent directly to a photodetector (PD) for pulse characterization. b) Optical pulses generated by the BOA and detected at PD when BOA is driven by nominally-rectangular unidirectional current pulses (low of 0 mA, high of 199.4 mA) at 10% duty cycle at repetition frequencies of 500 kHz (ma- genta), 350 kHz (blue), and 42 kHz (green). FIG. 3. Comparison of Faraday-rotation noise properties with FM and SOA-AM pumping modulation strategies. (a) SOA- AM’s demodulated polarization-noise PSD for different average pulsed pump powers at 70 µW of probe power measured before the atoms and pump duty cycle of 10%. Faded curves show raw data; solid curves show fits to equation.4 obtained using a maximum-likelihood estimator, following [48], excluding line noise at 50 Hz and its harmonics, and the 3 kHz to 4 kHz band. The shaded region indicates the uncertainty estimated via bootstrapping (500 iterations). Orange, purple and green curves show FM, AOM-AM and SOA-AM pumping, respectively, with pump- ing duty cycle 10 % and generating equal spin polarization (see text).
